food_name: Oyster with Chrysanthemum Egg (Hàu Trứng Cúc)
region_of_origin: Vietnam (served in Singapore Vietnamese restaurant context)
food_subgroup: Shellfish dishes with egg — oyster-based cooked preparation

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the specific Vietnamese oyster-with-egg preparation. FOODON_03301710 (oyster food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers oysters as a commodity. No specific code exists for the cooked oyster-with-egg Vietnamese dish; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Vietnamese oyster-with-egg preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving consists of a minimum of 4 oysters prepared with egg, typically weighing approximately 200 g including the egg coating and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Oysters, cooked (USDA FoodData Central, SR Legacy) and Egg, whole, cooked (USDA FoodData Central, SR Legacy) — composite estimate for oyster-with-egg dish
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct nutrient entry exists for the Vietnamese Hàu Trứng Cúc preparation in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked oysters (FDC ID 175159) and whole cooked egg (FDC ID 173424), combined in an approximate 60:40 oyster-to-egg ratio by weight, with minor contributions from fish sauce and sesame oil. Values represent a reasonable composite estimate per 100 g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of shellfish and mixed seafood-egg preparations — analogous estimate based on oysters and egg; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hàu Trứng Cúc. GI estimated at 18 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for oysters and cooked egg, both of which are very low-GI foods with minimal glycemic carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as (4.2 g carbohydrate × 18 / 100) × 2 = approximately 1.5 g GL. This is consistent with the low-GI, low-GL profile of shellfish-and-egg dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and natural oyster sodium
hypertension.guidance: Each 200 g serving provides approximately 640 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced fish sauce to keep sodium within the recommended 2000 mg/day ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 18 and a glycemic load of ~1.5 per serving, this dish has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content from oysters and egg, balanced by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Oysters provide beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and zinc, but the combined cholesterol from oysters and egg (~290 mg per serving) warrants moderation for individuals with established cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hàu Trứng Cúc is a Vietnamese dish consisting of fresh oysters cooked together with beaten egg, typically pan-fried or lightly steamed, and seasoned with fish sauce, sesame oil, and spring onions. The name translates literally as 'oyster chrysanthemum egg', referring to the chrysanthemum-like pattern formed by the egg as it sets around the oysters during cooking. The dish is served in portions of a minimum of four oysters per serving. Texturally, the oysters are tender and briny, while the egg provides a soft, silky coating. The flavour profile is savoury and umami-rich, with the natural sweetness of fresh oysters complemented by the aromatic fish sauce and sesame oil. In Singapore, this dish is served at Vietnamese restaurants and represents the Vietnamese coastal culinary tradition of combining shellfish with egg. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of high-quality protein (~10.8 g per 100 g), exceptionally rich in zinc (~28 mg per 100 g, supporting immune function), vitamin B12 (~8.5 mcg per 100 g, important for neurological health), and iron (~4.8 mg per 100 g). The glycemic index is very low (~18), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium content is moderate (~320 mg per 100 g) due to fish sauce, and individuals with hypertension should be mindful of portion size. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free but contains shellfish and egg allergens.

confidence_reason: No direct nutrient entry for Hàu Trứng Cúc exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked oysters and whole cooked egg in an approximate 60:40 ratio. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and based on approved sources, but the exact recipe proportions and cooking method at the specific restaurant are unknown.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy cooked oyster (FDC ID 175159) and cooked whole egg (FDC ID 173424) in an approximate 60:40 ratio by weight. Minor contributions from fish sauce (~5 g per 100 g dish) and sesame oil (~2 g per 100 g dish) are incorporated into the sodium and fat estimates. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as oysters and eggs contain only negligible naturally occurring trans fats below detection threshold; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central source data. 3. GI of 18 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific Vietnamese preparation was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite; these are reasonable approximations but should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis if this record is used for clinical purposes. 5. The dish is classified under Singapore cuisine context (served at a Singapore Vietnamese restaurant) but the origin is Vietnamese; cuisine field reflects Vietnamese origin. 6. Halal status is marked false as oysters are considered non-halal under strict Shafi'i interpretation followed by many Singaporean Muslims, though other madhabs permit them; this should be verified with the specific restaurant. 7. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values from USDA SR Legacy; no -1 sentinel values remain in this record.
